params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears. Completed Warmup graph with 3 categories, even values. Key visible: Each ⭐ = 2. Apples=8 (4 symbols), Bananas=6 (3 symbols), Oranges=10 (5 symbols).
Data table appears alongside graph, showing same Favorite Fruits values: Apples=8, Bananas=6, Oranges=10.

steps.text: You already read this graph in Warmup. You counted 5 symbols for Oranges. Here's the thing: each symbol shows 2 items, not 1. This is called a SCALED picture graph.
But HOW do you know each symbol shows 2? Let's find out.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Scale of 2. Favorite Fruits data: Apples=8 (4 symbols), Bananas=6 (3 symbols), Oranges=10 (5 symbols). Key visible.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values in numeric display mode.

params.description: Key area highlighted showing 'Each ⭐ = 2'.

steps.text: Every graph has a KEY. The key is like a decoder. It tells you what each symbol MEANS.
This key says: each star equals 2. That '2' is called the SCALE.
Scale of 1 means each symbol shows 1 item. So what does scale of 2 mean?
This graph has a scale of 2. What does that mean?

validator.description: Student selected 'Each symbol shows 2 items' — correct definition of scale

steps.text: Scale of 2 means each symbol represents 2 items. The KEY tells you the scale. Always check the key first.

System-generated 1:1 scale graph appears on right. Same Books Read data, but each book gets its own symbol. Key: Each ⭐ = 1. James shows 12 symbols. Both graphs visible simultaneously.

steps.text: Here's the same data — but with scale of 1. Every book gets its OWN symbol.
Look at James. Your graph shows 6 symbols. The scale-of-1 graph shows 12 symbols. Same number of books!
Which graph uses fewer symbols to show the same data?

validator.description: Student clicked scale of 2 graph — correct

steps.text: Scale of 2 — just 6 symbols instead of 12 for James. Same data, fewer symbols. That's why we use scales.
Now let's use YOUR graph to answer some questions.

params.description: Student's 1:2 graph returns to center position.

elements.description: Student's completed vertical picture graph at scale of 2, now centered. Scale of 1 comparison graph removed.

validator.description: Student clicked scale of 1 graph on first attempt

steps.text: Look at how many symbols each graph needs to show James's books.

validator.description: Student clicked scale of 1 graph on second attempt

steps.text: Your graph shows 6 symbols for James. The other graph shows 12 symbols. Which uses fewer symbols?

validator.description: Student clicked scale of 1 graph three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: James row pulses on scale of 2 graph, showing 6 symbols

steps.text: Let me show you. Your graph on the left uses scale of 2 — James has 6 symbols. The right graph uses scale of 1 — James has 12 symbols. Same 12 books, but your graph needs fewer symbols. Click your graph on the left.

elements.description: Student's completed 1:2 graph on left side. Maya=4.5, James=6, Sofia=3.5, Aiden=5.5 symbols.
System-generated 1:1 graph on right side. Same data with scale of 1. Both graphs visible for comparison.

steps.text: Use your graph to answer: Who read the most books?
Click on the category with the most books.

validator.description: Student clicked James — correct (12 books = 6 symbols)

steps.text: James read the most — 12 books. 6 symbols, each worth 2.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. James column highlighted as having the most with 6 symbols = 12 books.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the symbols in each column. Which one has the most?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count James's symbols: 6. Each symbol is worth 2 books, so that's 12 total. Check the others—who has more than 6 symbols?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System counts symbols for each person: Maya 5, James 6, Sofia 4, Aiden 3. James column highlights as tallest.

steps.text: This is tricky, so let me show you. Count the symbols: Maya has 5, James has 6, Sofia has 4, Aiden has 3. James has the most symbols—6 symbols times 2 books each equals 12 books. Click James.

elements.description: Student's completed vertical picture graph. Click category tool active. All 4 categories visible.

params.description: James (6 symbols) and Aiden (5.5 symbols) highlighted for addition question.

steps.text: How many books did James and Aiden read IN ALL?
How many books did James and Aiden read IN ALL?

validator.description: Student answered 23 — correct (12 + 11 = 23)

steps.text: 23 books in all. James read 12, Aiden read 11. 12 plus 11 is 23.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. James and Aiden highlighted. Combined total of 23 books confirmed.

validator.description: Student selected 11 — counted only Aiden's books, not both students combined

steps.text: You found Aiden's books. The question asks for IN ALL — both James AND Aiden together. Add their books.

validator.description: Student selected 17 — added the number of symbols instead of counting each symbol as 2 books

steps.text: Here's a hint: look at the key. Each symbol stands for 2 books. Count James's symbols, multiply by 2. Do the same for Aiden. Then add.

validator.description: Student selected 28 — added all four students' books instead of just James and Aiden

steps.text: You added all the students. The question only asks for James AND Aiden. Count their books, then add those two amounts together.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System highlights James (6 symbols × 2 = 12), then Aiden (5.5 symbols × 2 = 11), then shows 12 + 11 = 23

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. James has 6 symbols. Each symbol is 2 books, so 6 times 2 is 12 books. Aiden has 5 and a half symbols. That's 11 books. Now we add: 12 plus 11 equals 23 books in all. See how that works?

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. James (6 symbols = 12) and Aiden (5.5 symbols = 11) highlighted. Multiple choice options for total visible.

params.description: All highlighting cleared for new two-step problem.

steps.text: Here's a two-step problem. How many MORE books did Maya and Sofia read together than James?
Step 1: How many books did Maya and Sofia read together?

params.description: Maya (4.5 symbols) and Sofia (3.5 symbols) highlighted for Step 1 addition.

steps.text: How many books did Maya and Sofia read in all?

validator.description: Student answered 16 — correct (9 + 7 = 16)

steps.text: 16 books together.

params.description: Step 1 result '16' displayed. James highlighted for Step 2 comparison.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Step 1 complete showing Maya + Sofia = 16. James highlighted for Step 2.

validator.description: Student selected 9 — only counted Maya's books, didn't add Sofia

params.description: Both Maya and Sofia remain highlighted to show we need both categories.

steps.text: We need Maya AND Sofia together. Count Maya's 9 books, then add Sofia's 7 books.

validator.description: Student selected 12 — added James instead of Sofia, or miscounted

params.description: Maya and Sofia highlighted, James dimmed to clarify which two categories to add.

steps.text: Look at Maya and Sofia only. Maya has 9 books, Sofia has 7 books. Add those two amounts.

validator.description: Student selected 19 — added all three readers or made a counting error

params.description: Only Maya and Sofia highlighted, other categories dimmed.

steps.text: The question asks for Maya and Sofia together, not everyone. Count just those two rows: 9 plus 7.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System counts Maya's 9 symbols, then Sofia's 7 symbols, showing 9 + 7 = 16.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Maya read 9 books—count her row. Sofia read 7 books—count her row. Together that's 9 plus 7, which equals 16 books.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Maya and Sofia highlighted for Step 1 addition. Multiple choice options visible.

steps.text: Step 2: Compare James to that total.
James read 12. Maya and Sofia read 16. Who read more, and by how many?

validator.description: Student selected 'Maya and Sofia read 4 more' — correct (16 - 12 = 4)

steps.text: Maya and Sofia read 4 more books together than James. 16 minus 12 is 4.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Two-step problem complete. Maya and Sofia's combined 16 compared to James's 12, difference of 4 confirmed.

validator.description: Student selected 'James read 4 more' — reversed the comparison direction

params.description: James (12) highlighted with Maya + Sofia total (16) displayed for comparison.

steps.text: Check which number is bigger: James has 12, Maya and Sofia have 16. 16 is more than 12, so Maya and Sofia read more.

validator.description: Student selected 'James read 28 more' — added instead of subtracting (16 + 12)

params.description: Visual emphasizes comparing 16 to 12, not adding them.

steps.text: The question asks how many MORE—that means subtract to find the difference. Take the bigger number 16 and subtract 12.

validator.description: Student selected 'They read the same' — didn't compare the amounts correctly

params.description: James row and combined total shown side-by-side to emphasize the difference.

steps.text: Look closely at the amounts: James has 12 books, Maya and Sofia have 16 books together. Those aren't equal—16 is more than 12.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System shows Maya + Sofia total (16) and James (12), then highlights the 4-symbol difference between them.

steps.text: Let me show you. Maya and Sofia together have 16 books. James has 12 books. To find how many more, we subtract: 16 minus 12 equals 4. Maya and Sofia read 4 more books than James.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Step 1 result '16' displayed. James highlighted. Step 2 multiple choice options visible.

steps.text: You made scaled picture graphs. You know each symbol equals 2. You know half-symbols show 1. Let's see what you know.

elements.description: Final completed Books Read graph remains briefly visible before transitioning to Exit Check.
